criteria: The agent has read Section 1 to identify that logical preference consistency in LLMs is evaluated based on three fundamental properties: transitivity, commutativity, and negation invariance.
The agent has read Section 2.1 to define the transitivity metric which calculates the probability that a randomly sampled sub-graph of size K contains no cycles.

The agent has read Section 2.2 to extract the commutativity metric which evaluates whether the model's judgment remains consistent when the order of compared items is reversed to test positional bias.
The agent has read Section 2.3 to identify the negation invariance metric which assesses if the model can correctly reverse its judgment when explicitly prompted with a negated relationship.

The evaluation plan explicitly outlines testing the three core logical properties by measuring transitivity over sub-graphs, commutativity via swapped item orders, and negation invariance using negated relations.
The code correctly implements Algorithm 1 (from Appendix A) to construct a directed preference relation graph by parsing the LLM's outputs and setting edges based on probability thresholds
The implementation accurately executes Algorithm 2 using Depth-First Search (DFS) to detect cycles within randomly sampled sub-graphs of size K to compute the transitivity metric.
The codebase includes logic to calculate commutativity by programmatically swapping the input items (A and B) in the prompt and verifying if the resulting preference matrix is symmetric.
The evaluation script correctly implements negation invariance by sending paired standard and negated prompts to the LLM and asserting that the relational outputs are perfectly inverted.

The LLM inference script for computing transitivity, commutativity, and negation invariance executes successfully and correctly parses the binary choices (A/B or Yes/No) without raising formatting exceptions.
The agent has read Section 3.1 to identify that model reliability is measured by self-agreement through Monte Carlo Sampling of Chain-of-Thought reasoning outputs.
The agent has read Section 3.2 to extract the finding that Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting does not generally improve logical consistency and can sometimes decrease transitivity.
The agent has read Section 3.3 to observe the strong correlation between transitivity and self-agreement, establishing transitivity as a proxy for evaluating the local robustness of LLMs.
The agent has read Section 3.3 to note that commutativity correlates strongly with human preferences, indicating that lack of commutativity often reflects a strong positional bias.
The experimental setup details a plan to measure model reliability using self-agreement from Monte Carlo Sampling and explicitly compares the impact of standard prompting versus Chain-of-Thought.
The inference loop is correctly configured to perform Monte Carlo Sampling by generating 10 Chain-of-Thought responses per query with a temperature of 0.7.
The code accurately computes the self-agreement metric by implementing a majority voting function across the sampled CoT outputs to determine the final stable judgement.
The Monte Carlo sampling pipeline for calculating self-agreement runs to completion for 10 iterations per query without encountering context length limits or Out-of-Memory (OOM) errors.

The evaluation results for the consistency metrics (transitivity, commutativity, negation invariance) on the SummEval, NovelEval, and CaTeRS datasets must match the values reported in Table 1 for the corresponding models.

The implementation results must reproduce the finding that Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting leads to inconsistent behavior, specifically observing a decrease in transitivity across the evaluated datasets as shown in Table 1.
The codebase must validate the strong positive correlation between transitivity and self-agreement as depicted in the scatter plots of Figure 4.
The agent has read Section 4 to understand the REPAIR framework, which refines noisy pairwise comparisons using rank aggregation and logically extrapolates additional conflict-free comparisons.
The agent has read Section 4.1 to point out that the win-loss rate is utilized for rank estimation within REPAIR due to its simplicity and robustness with partial or sparse comparisons.

The agent has read Section 4.2 to extract the core finding that models trained on the REPAIR-ed dataset achieve significant gains in logical consistency—specifically transitivity and commutativity—while successfully maintaining strong alignment with human preferences.
The training plan specifies using the Summarize from Feedback dataset and applying synthetic noise to simulate self-contradictory preferences for testing the REPAIR framework.

The execution strategy for REPAIR clearly details using the win-loss rate for rank estimation on the noisy data and subsequently augmenting it with both conflict-free and negated pairwise comparisons.
The experimental methodology dictates evaluating the REPAIR framework by instruction-tuning the Llama-3-8B model across raw, perturbed, and augmented data conditions.
The data preprocessing script correctly simulates real-world noisy preferences by randomly selecting and flipping the relational labels of exactly 10% of the training instances.
The codebase accurately implements the win-loss rate rank estimation by calculating (wins - losses) / total_comparisons for each item to construct a globally sorted ranking.

The data augmentation script successfully extrapolates the estimated global ranking into a complete set of conflict-free pairwise comparisons and automatically generates their corresponding negated samples.
The rank estimation script computes the win-loss rates and constructs the fully sorted rankings from the sparse, perturbed data without zero-division or index-out-of-bounds errors.
The REPAIR data augmentation pipeline executes flawlessly to generate the expanded conflict-free dataset and negated relations without creating duplicate or logically contradicting pairs.
The LoRA instruction-tuning script for the Llama-3-8B model executes successfully on the REPAIR-ed dataset without any Out-of-Memory (OOM) or tensor shape mismatch errors during gradient updates.
The execution results must demonstrate that REPAIR-ed models achieve significant gains in transitivity and commutativity while maintaining alignment with human preferences, matching Table 3.

The output evaluation must confirm that training on negated relations enhances negation invariance, while also noting the potential trade-off (forgetting effect) on other logical properties reported in Section 4.2.

The agent has read Section 5 to observe that LLMs with higher transitivity and commutativity yield better ranking performance and require less calibration when used as logical operators in the PairS algorithm.
The downstream evaluation plan mandates testing the consistency-enhanced LLMs as logical pairwise comparators within the PairS rank aggregation algorithm.
The testing protocol for the PairS algorithm includes running the evaluation both with and without positional bias calibration to practically demonstrate the benefits of improved commutativity.
The training loop correctly configures the LoRA hyperparameters for Llama-3-8B, strictly setting the learning rate to 5e-5, rank (r) to 16, and alpha to 64 as specified in the appendix.
The codebase successfully implements the PairS merge-sort algorithm where standard comparison operators are replaced by LLM inference calls.

The implementation accurately applies positional bias calibration within PairS by extracting and mathematically averaging the evaluation probabilities across both pairwise order permutations (A->B and B->A).

The downstream PairS sorting algorithm runs to completion, dynamically invoking the LLM as a comparator and applying positional bias calibration without halting due to recursion limits or execution timeouts.
The results from the PairS algorithm integration must replicate the performance gains in Table 4, showing that consistency-enhanced models achieve higher Spearman correlation scores.
The implementation results must demonstrate that more commutative models require less calibration in the PairS algorithm, effectively reproducing the calibration gain analysis presented in Table 4.
